consensus.note: Consensus supports provisional Loop decisions only; the Experiment Champion remains pending minimum Loops and synthesis.

normalized_preferences.rationale: B retains A's direct editability but gives much stronger hierarchy, working in-page orientation, contextual help, and a more actionable recovery presentation. A's task-card status is useful, but its all-open mobile form and heavy outlines increase scanning burden.

C's explicit step-by-step progression, save/resume controls, and compact review reduce cognitive load and make interruption handling clearer. B is visually clearer and has stronger immediate invalid-submission recovery; C's empty-step advancement and unconfirmed Clear action prevent high confidence.
C provides clearer progress, more focused task sequencing, stronger feedback, and explicit save/resume behaviour. A is fully operable but presents more simultaneous content and visual noise.

Candidate C wins on input_burden (tolerant validation vs candidate B's exact-fixture match on state/country/card/name/CSC), cognitive_load (one wizard step visible at a time vs a single-page with ~20 required fields), interruption_resumption (explicit Save/Clear controls and restored step position vs auto-only), and feedback_status (progress bar with narrated aria-valuetext plus polite status vs assertive-only error live region). Candidate B is slightly stronger on error message specificity but suffers from double-radio ARIA on shipping and autocomplete='off' on every input, both anti-patterns that penalise motor- and cognitively-impaired users. Screens: screenshots/candidate-c/01-initial-390x844.png vs screenshots/candidate-b/01-initial-390x844.png.

Candidate B wins on error_prevention_recovery (highly specific messages vs candidate A's generic 'Please enter a value'), feedback_status (polite aria-live + focused error summary vs A's misused assertive routine updates), latency_perception (180 ms placement vs A's 500 ms), destructive_actions (placementStarted flag + disabled-immediately vs A's disable-only-inside-callback), interruption_resumption (no payment-field leak vs A restoring card-name), navigation (section-nav pills vs no skip-link/no nav), and discoverability (visible order summary + numbered steps vs A's bottom-only summary). Candidate A retains marginal advantages in cognitive_load-per-view (fewer visible fields at once) but the missing skip-link, missing landmark aside, aria-live misuse, and payment leakage outweigh that. Screens: screenshots/candidate-b/01-initial-390x844.png vs screenshots/candidate-a/01-initial-390x844.png.

Candidate C wins across almost every lens: it has a narrated progress bar (feedback_status), explicit Save/Clear controls with restored step position (interruption_resumption), 120 ms placement with aria-busy narration (latency_perception), aria-disabled Place order + orderPlaced flag (destructive_actions), field-specific error messages routed to the correct step (error_prevention_recovery), single-active-continue wizard grouping (cognitive_load), skip-link present (discoverability/navigation), and no payment-field persistence (interruption_resumption). Candidate A has no skip-link, no landmark aside, misuses assertive aria-live, restores card-name on reload, and imposes a 500 ms placement delay. Screens: screenshots/candidate-c/01-initial-390x844.png vs screenshots/candidate-a/01-initial-390x844.png.

comparisons.rationale: B retains A's direct editability but gives much stronger hierarchy, working in-page orientation, contextual help, and a more actionable recovery presentation. A's task-card status is useful, but its all-open mobile form and heavy outlines increase scanning burden.

C's explicit step-by-step progression, save/resume controls, and compact review reduce cognitive load and make interruption handling clearer. B is visually clearer and has stronger immediate invalid-submission recovery; C's empty-step advancement and unconfirmed Clear action prevent high confidence.
C provides clearer progress, more focused task sequencing, stronger feedback, and explicit save/resume behaviour. A is fully operable but presents more simultaneous content and visual noise.

human_use.rationale: The Artifact is a human-operated mobile checkout. Its success depends on discoverability, navigation, input burden, error recovery, feedback, accessibility, touch ergonomics, interruption recovery, perceived latency, destructive-action safety, and cognitive load across cross-disability use.

raw.objective_reports: All pass and are not rank inputs for this qualitative comparison.

human_use.rationale: The Artifact is a human-operated mobile checkout. Its success depends on discoverability, navigation, input burden, error recovery, feedback, accessibility, touch ergonomics, interruption recovery, perceived latency, destructive-action safety, and cognitive load across cross-disability use.

raw.identity_note: No attempt was made to infer or reveal Track or model identity from bundle contents. Comparisons are strictly artifact-behaviour based.
raw.note: All three objective reports pass; qualitative preferences here do not override any objective gate. Pairwise preferences are advisory raw judge input; final aggregation is by panel median with dissent preserved.
